14.text: Speculative decoding keeps ordinary attention math but changes the control flow of inference: guess several tokens, then verify them in bulk.

15.text: Same names task, so the speedup story is about decoding strategy rather than about a different workload.

16.text: Sampling still matters because both the draft and target models are generating candidates inside the speculative loop.

17.text: Timing is central here: speculative decoding only matters if one target-model pass can replace many expensive autoregressive passes.

19.text: This lab uses two real models at once, because the key production idea is a cheap draft model feeding a more expensive target model.

20.text: Both models share the same basic transformer recipe; the interesting part is not architectural novelty but how they cooperate during decoding.

21.text: The probability math matters because speculative decoding lives or dies on correctly comparing what the draft wanted against what the target would allow.

23.text: Fix the run so the draft-vs-target story is reproducible enough to inspect and benchmark cleanly.

24.text: Fix PyTorch's random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

29.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script.

30.text: If the names dataset isn't cached locally yet, download it on the fly so the script works out of the box.

31.text: Only import the URL library when we actually need to download, keeping startup fast if the file already exists.

34.text: Fetch the dataset from GitHub and save it locally so future runs skip the download entirely.

36.text: Read the file line by line, stripping whitespace. Each line is one name that becomes a training document.

37.text: Shuffle to prevent memorizing alphabetical order.

38.text: Show how many names we loaded so we can verify the dataset is intact.

40.text: Build character vocabulary from unique characters in all names.

43.text: Display the character vocabulary size, including the special BOS/EOS token.

48.text: Max sequence length for all models, names rarely exceed 16 characters.

51.text: RMSNorm: cheaper than LayerNorm, just rescales by inverse root-mean-square.

52.text: Dimension parameter unused but kept for API consistency.

53.text: Call the parent class constructor to properly register this module in PyTorch's module hierarchy for parameter tracking.

54.text: Small epsilon prevents division by zero.

56.text: The forward method computes the RMSNorm normalization for the input tensor.

57.text: Scale x by inverse RMS, rsqrt computes 1/sqrt efficiently.

60.text: Causal (left-to-right) attention, both draft and target use the same architecture, just different sizes.

61.text: Constructor takes embedding dimension and number of heads.

62.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

63.text: Remember how many heads we're using so the forward pass can split the projected vectors into the right number of slices.

64.text: Calculate how many dimensions each head gets by dividing the total embedding size evenly among all heads.

65.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into query vectors for attention.

66.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into key vectors for attention.

67.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into value vectors for attention.

70.text: Run the complete attention computation: project to Q/K/V, compute scores, mask future positions, and blend values.

71.text: Unpack batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

72.text: Project to queries and reshape: (B, T, C) → (B, n_head, T, head_dim). This splits the embedding into independent heads.

73.text: Reshape keys into the same multi-head format as queries, splitting the embedding dimension across independent heads.

74.text: Reshape values into multi-head format. Unlike Q and K which compute scores, values carry the content that attention retrieves.

75.text: Scaled dot-product attention: Q·K^T / sqrt(d). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating in high dimensions.

76.text: Upper triangular mask: prevents each position from attending to future positions. This is what makes it causal.

77.text: Fill future positions with -inf so softmax gives them zero weight.

78.text: Softmax over the key dimension gives attention weights.

79.text: Weighted sum of values, then reshape back to (B, T, C).

80.text: Output projection mixes information across heads.

83.text: Feed-forward network: expand then contract, with nonlinearity in between.

84.text: Store the embedding dimension so the two Linear layers know their input and 4x-expanded output sizes.

85.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

86.text: Expand to 4x width, the MLP's "thinking space".

89.text: Run the input through the expand-ReLU-contract sequence and return the result.

90.text: ReLU sandwiched between two linear layers.

93.text: A transformer block: attention + MLP with residual connections.

95.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

96.text: RMSNorm before attention stabilizes the input scale, so the projection matrices see consistent activation magnitudes.

97.text: The multi-head causal self-attention layer that lets each token gather information from all previous positions in the sequence.

98.text: RMSNorm before the feed-forward network, ensuring the MLP sees well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

99.text: The position-wise feed-forward network that processes each token independently through an expand-ReLU-contract pattern.

101.text: Run input through attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

103.text: Add the MLP's refinement to the residual stream, preserving the original signal so the layer can only improve representations.

104.text: Pass the updated hidden states back to the caller. These flow into the next transformer block or the final projection.

107.text: The full GPT model, used for both draft and target, just with different sizes.

108.text: Parameterized constructor: embedding size, heads, and layers are configurable for creating differently-sized models.

109.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

110.text: Token embedding: maps token IDs to dense vectors.

111.text: Position embedding: maps positions to dense vectors.

112.text: Normalize the sum of token and position embeddings before entering the transformer stack, stabilizing the first layer's input.

113.text: Stack of transformer blocks, the draft model gets 1, the target gets 2.

114.text: Language model head: projects hidden states to vocabulary logits.

115.text: Walk through every submodule and apply the weight initialization function, ensuring all layers start with well-scaled parameters.

118.text: Defines how weights are initialized. Called once per module by self.apply() during model construction.

119.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers.

120.text: Small Gaussian initialization (std=0.08) prevents exploding activations.

122.text: Forward pass: token IDs in, logits out.

124.text: Convert each input token ID into a dense vector by indexing into the learned embedding table.

125.text: Fetch a learned position vector for each slot in the sequence so the model knows the ordering of tokens.

126.text: Add token + position embeddings, then normalize.

127.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block in sequence, progressively refining the representations.

128.text: Run one transformer block, updating the hidden representations for this layer.

129.text: Project to vocabulary logits, one score per token in the vocabulary.

135.text: CPU for this demo, the speedup story is cleaner on GPU where memory-boundedness is stark.

138.text: Draft model: 32-dim embeddings, 4 heads, 1 layer. Small and fast, but less accurate. It will propose tokens cheaply.

140.text: Target model: 64-dim embeddings, 4 heads, 2 layers. Better quality, but 4x slower per forward pass. This is the model whose distribution we want.

143.text: Shared training function, both models train the same way, just with different architectures.

144.text: Print model name and parameter count.

145.text: Adam optimizer with the standard hyperparameters.

146.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

147.text: Pick the next training example by cycling through the shuffled name list with a modulo index.

148.text: Tokenize: BOS + characters + BOS (BOS doubles as EOS).

150.text: Input: all tokens except the last.

151.text: Target: all tokens except the first (shifted by one).

154.text: Zero out gradients from the previous step.

156.text: Scale the learning rate down linearly from its initial value toward zero as training progresses, for smoother convergence.

157.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses the updated value on the next step.

158.text: Assign the computed decayed learning rate value to this parameter group's learning rate entry.

159.text: Apply the Adam optimizer update rule to adjust all model parameters using gradients.

160.text: Print a status update at regular intervals and on the first step, so we can watch the loss decrease during training.

161.text: Show the current training step number and loss value so we can track convergence at a glance.

164.text: Instantiate the small draft model with 32-dim embeddings and 1 layer, then move it to the compute device for training.

165.text: Create the target model, same architecture class, different size.

167.text: Run the standard training loop on the small draft model so it learns basic character-level patterns for proposing tokens.

168.text: Train the target model separately, in production these would be pre-trained independently.

173.text: Temperature 0.5: moderate creativity. Lower values make output more deterministic.

176.text: Disable gradient computation for inference, saves memory and speeds things up.

177.text: Helper: run a model on tokens and return the probability distribution for every position.

179.text: Wrap tokens in a tensor, keeping only the last block_size tokens (sliding window).

180.text: Forward pass and apply temperature. Index [0] removes the batch dimension.

181.text: Softmax converts logits to probabilities. Returns (T, V), one distribution per position.

187.text: Standard autoregressive generation: one token, one forward pass, repeat. This is the baseline we want to beat.

189.text: Start with just the BOS token.

192.text: Run the model on the full token sequence and return only the last position's probability distribution for next-token prediction.

193.text: Sample one token from the distribution.

194.text: The BOS token doubles as an end-of-sequence marker. When sampled, it signals that the name is complete.

195.text: End-of-sequence detected, so stop generating further tokens for this name.

196.text: Append the new token and continue.

197.text: Hand back the generated token sequence with the leading BOS removed, since it was just a start signal, not part of the name.

203.text: The heart of the lab: speculative decoding with draft model draft and target model target. K=4 means the draft model guesses 4 tokens ahead.

229.text: Statistics tracking: how many tokens drafted, accepted, and how many forward passes each model ran.

231.text: Disable PyTorch's gradient tracking during inference since we don't need backpropagation, saving memory and compute.

234.text: Copy current tokens, the draft model will extend this speculatively.

235.text: Track each drafted token and its probability under the draft model.

236.text: Speculatively generate K tokens using the cheap draft model. These guesses will be verified against the target in one batch.

237.text: Stop if we've hit the length limit.

238.text: Break out of the draft loop.

239.text: Get draft model's next-token probabilities. This is cheap because the draft model is small.

240.text: Keep a running count of how many forward passes the draft model has executed, for the efficiency comparison later.

241.text: Sample a token from the draft distribution.

242.text: Record the token and its draft probability q(x), we'll need q(x) for the acceptance ratio.

243.text: Append the newly sampled token to the draft sequence so the next draft step can condition on it.

245.text: Edge case: no tokens were drafted.

246.text: Edge case: if no tokens were drafted (e.g., length limit hit immediately), skip the verification step entirely.

248.text: Record how many tokens the draft model proposed.

252.text: The key efficiency win: the target model scores ALL drafted positions in ONE forward pass. Autoregressive decoding would need K separate forward passes. Since inference is memory-bound (reading weights dominates), one pass costs almost the same as K.

253.text: Count target forward passes, should be much fewer than autoregressive.

256.text: Track how many consecutive drafts get accepted in this round.

257.text: Verify each drafted token against the target model's distribution.

260.text: Map draft position to target's output position.

261.text: Make sure the draft position maps to a valid index in the target's output tensor.

262.text: The position is beyond what the target model computed, so exit the verification loop.

263.text: Get the target model's probability p(x) for the exact token the draft proposed.

266.text: Zero draft probability means the draft model would never have picked this token, reject.

268.text: The acceptance probability: min(1, p(x)/q(x)). If the target likes this token at least as much as the draft, always accept. If the target likes it less, accept proportionally. This ratio is what guarantees the output distribution matches the target exactly.

270.text: Coin flip: accept with the computed probability.

272.text: Token accepted, add to the final sequence.

273.text: Bump the accepted token counter. We'll compare accepted vs. drafted to compute the acceptance rate.

274.text: Special case: accepted token is BOS (end of sequence).

275.text: Remove BOS from the output and stop generating.

276.text: Update stats (don't count BOS as accepted).

277.text: Bundle the final token sequence with the draft/target statistics so the caller can analyze both quality and efficiency.

280.text: Retrieve the target model's full probability distribution at the rejected position, needed for the correction sampling step.

281.text: Reconstruct the draft context up to the rejected position.

282.text: Sum the corrected distribution to check if it has positive mass. If it's all zeros, we'll fall back to the raw target distribution.

283.text: If there's positive mass in the adjusted distribution...

284.text: Normalize to a proper probability distribution.

286.text: Fallback: if adjustment zeroes everything out, use the target's raw distribution.

288.text: If the sampled token is BOS (end-of-sequence marker), the name is done and we should stop generating further tokens.

289.text: Record the final counts and return early since the name ended with the corrected token.

290.text: Package the completed sequence and return it to the caller along with the generation statistics.

291.text: Add the corrected token to the output sequence. This token was sampled from the adjusted distribution to match the target's behavior.

292.text: After rejection, restart the draft loop from the new position. Don't try to reuse remaining drafts.

294.text: Python for-else: this runs if the loop completed without break, meaning ALL K tokens were accepted.

296.text: When all K drafts are accepted, we get a bonus: the target already computed the probability distribution for the position after the last draft.

297.text: Bounds check on the bonus position.

298.text: Look up what the target model would predict at the bonus position, which we get for free since the target already computed it.

299.text: Sample a bonus token, a free extra token from the target with no additional cost.

300.text: Don't append BOS (that ends the sequence).

301.text: Add this free bonus token to the sequence. It cost zero extra forward passes since it came from the target's existing computation.

303.text: Update accepted count at end of round.

305.text: Package the completed token sequence together with the acceptance statistics for the final benchmark comparison.

313.text: Switch draft model to evaluation mode (disables dropout if any).

314.text: Switch target model to evaluation mode.

318.text: Utility: time a generation function over n_samples runs.

319.text: Print a label identifying which generation method is about to be timed, so the benchmark output is easy to read.

320.text: Record the wall-clock start time so we can measure total generation duration.

321.text: Run the generation function for all samples, collecting both the outputs and the total wall-clock time.

322.text: Record the end time so we can compute the total elapsed duration and per-sample average.

324.text: Bundle the generated samples together with the elapsed time so the caller can analyze both output quality and speed.

327.text: Benchmark 1: standard autoregressive decoding with the target model (the baseline).

328.text: One forward pass per token, the slowest approach.

332.text: Zero out the counters for drafted tokens, accepted tokens, and forward passes before aggregating across all samples.

333.text: List to collect results with stats.

335.text: Benchmark 2: speculative decoding (draft proposes, target verifies).

336.text: K=4: draft guesses 4 tokens per round.

337.text: Lambda collects both the tokens and the acceptance statistics from each generation.

340.text: Add up each statistic category (drafted, accepted, target passes, draft passes) across all generated samples.

341.text: Add this sample's individual stat values into the running totals for the final summary.

343.text: Benchmark 3: draft-only autoregressive (for reference, fast but lower quality).

344.text: Uses the small draft model alone.

350.text: Acceptance rate: what fraction of draft tokens the target model approved. Higher means the draft model closely matches the target.

351.text: Average target forward passes per sample, should be much less than the average token count.

352.text: Average draft forward passes per sample.

354.text: Average tokens generated speculatively (should match autoregressive closely).

356.text: Print the statistics section header before showing acceptance rates and forward pass counts.

357.text: Display what fraction of the draft model's proposed tokens the target model accepted, showing how well the models agree.

358.text: Compare forward pass counts, this is the efficiency win.

360.text: Wall-clock speedup ratio: how much faster speculative is.

361.text: Token counts should be similar, the distribution is mathematically identical.

386.text: Show actual generated names from each method.

388.text: The names generated by standard autoregressive decoding with the full target model, our quality baseline.

389.text: The names generated by speculative decoding, which should match the target's distribution exactly despite being faster.
